original_abstract:
GROMACS is a cornerstone of modern biomolecular simulation, having evolved over twenty years from a tool for small-scale efficiency into a high-performance engine capable of leveraging the world’s largest supercomputers. This evolution has been driven by a relentless focus on absolute performance and the integration of parallelism at every architectural level. 

In this work, we detail the strategies used to achieve this scaling, specifically highlighting GROMACS Release 4.6. This version introduces wide-ranging SIMD acceleration, GPU offloading, and a hybrid parallelization model using OpenMP and MPI to manage computation within and between nodes. These advancements required a fundamental reimagining of molecular simulation algorithms, such as neighbor-searching, to suit modern hardware. We explore the transition toward exascale computing—emphasizing the need for fine-grained task parallelism—and discuss the rigorous software engineering practices, including peer code review and continuous integration, essential for maintaining a scientific project of this scale and complexity.
humanized_abstract:
One of the largest open source molecular simulation software programs in the world, GROMACS has become a standard in the scientific community for simulating biological molecules over the past twenty years. Starting from a small efficiency code for few dozen molecules, it has developed into a flexible and highly scalable high performance computing engine that uses some of the worlds largest supercomputers to simulate larger biological systems.

This paper describes the strategies that we employ to achieve the scaling, primarily using GROMACS Release 4.6. This version has substantial SIMD support for all calculations, full GPU offloading, and a hybrid OpenMP-MPI strategy to divide work within a node and between nodes. We describe in some detail the changes that had to be made to low-level parts of the simulation code (such as the neighbor search) in order to accommodate the new capabilities of the computer hardware. We put the transition to the exascale computing regime into perspective, which will require an even more fine-grained task parallelism, and describe the large amounts of software engineering that are needed to sustain a development effort on a scientific project of this size.
